ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Singaporean roasted chicken rice; closest match is a generic chicken rice dish concept.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers chicken with rice as a composite dish; no specific code exists for the Singaporean roasted chicken rice preparation.

preparation_method: seasoning with soy sauce and sesame oil

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard hawker-centre serving of roasted chicken rice in Singapore typically comprises one plate of fragrant rice (approximately 200 g) topped with sliced roasted chicken (approximately 120–150 g), accompanied by cucumber slices, chili sauce, and dark soy sauce, totalling approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Chicken Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Roasted Chicken Rice (CR ID F05100112). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (cooked, Asian-style) as analogous food basis
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean roasted chicken rice as a composite dish. GI of 68 is estimated using white rice (cooked, Asian-style) as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for jasmine/long-grain white rice (GI 64–72, median ~68). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 57.5) / 100 ≈ 39.1, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 23.91 × 3.5 = 83.7 g; however, rice portion (~200 g) contributes approximately 57.5 g available carbohydrate. GL reported on full 350 g plate basis using total carbohydrate: (68 × 83.7) / 100 = 56.9; conservative estimate of 39.1 used reflecting rice-dominant carbohydrate fraction. Input record GL of 16.3 appears to be per-100 g basis: (68 × 23.91) / 100 = 16.3, confirmed consistent.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 440 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,540 mg sodium, representing over 67% of the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and chili sauce, avoid adding dark soy sauce at the table, and limit portion size to no more than 200 g per meal to keep sodium intake below 900 mg per sitting.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice dominates carbohydrate content at 23.9 g/100 g with a medium GI of 68 and low dietary fibre of 0.91 g/100 g, producing a high glycemic load per serving that drives rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request brown rice or reduce the rice portion to half a plate, increase vegetable accompaniments such as cucumber and steamed greens to add fibre, and avoid sweet chili sauce to limit added sugars, in line with ADA dietary guidelines for glycemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 440 mg/100 g and the presence of chicken fat and soy-based sauces contribute to saturated fat and sodium load; fat at 5.34 g/100 g is moderate but the sodium burden is the primary cardiovascular concern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose skinless chicken breast to reduce saturated fat, request rice cooked with minimal chicken fat and broth, limit soy sauce additions, and keep the serving to 250 g or less to manage combined sodium and saturated fat intake per the American Heart Association dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined white rice with low fibre (0.91 g/100 g) promotes glycemic-load-related systemic inflammation, and sodium at 440 mg/100 g exceeds the RA-relevant threshold of 400 mg/100 g, potentially worsening tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request brown rice, reduce soy sauce to lower sodium, choose skinless chicken breast to reduce saturated fat, and add steamed vegetables or extra cucumber to introduce antioxidants; those sensitive to nightshades should avoid chili sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Chicken Rice (烧鸡饭, Nasi Ayam Panggang) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of fragrant white rice cooked in chicken broth and chicken fat, served with slices of Chinese-style roasted chicken that has been marinated and roasted with soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and ginger. The rice is aromatic and slightly glossy from the cooking fat, while the chicken is tender with a caramelised, savoury skin. The dish is typically accompanied by sliced cucumber, a ginger-garlic dipping sauce, sweet dark soy sauce, and a spicy chili sauce. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 175 kcal, 7.74 g protein, 5.34 g fat, and 23.91 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 440 mg and dietary fibre at only 0.91 g. The medium glycemic index of 68 and low fibre content make this dish a moderate-to-high glycemic load meal, particularly relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The sodium content exceeds 400 mg/100 g, making portion control important for those with cardiovascular disease or hypertension. Roasted chicken rice is a staple of Singapore hawker culture, commonly consumed as a working lunch or dinner, and is widely available at hawker centres, food courts, and coffee shops across Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Roasted Chicken Rice (CR ID F05100112) with confidence 0.95. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (white rice, Asian-style, GI 68) with documented basis. Multilingual names verified for Singaporean Chinese cuisine category. Saturated fat estimated at 1.5 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for roasted chicken with skin and white rice composite; all other micronutrients set to -1 as HPB entry does not report detailed micronutrient breakdown.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.5 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for a composite of roasted chicken (with skin) and white rice, as the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Roasted Chicken Rice does not report these micronutrients, and no supplementary approved source was available to fill these values with sufficient confidence for this composite dish. 3. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (Asian-style, cooked) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Singaporean roasted chicken rice as a composite dish was found in approved sources. 4. Glycemic load of 16.3 per 100 g is consistent with input record and confirmed by calculation: (68 × 23.91) / 100 = 16.3. Per-serving GL (350 g plate) = approximately 56.9. 5. Halal dietary tag preserved from input; dish is commonly prepared as halal in Singapore hawker context. 6. Nightshade caution noted in rheumatoid arthritis additional condition for chili sauce sensitivity.
